Mendeleev arranged elements by increasing atomic mass and, crucially, left gaps for undiscovered elements, predicting their properties with remarkable accuracy.33 The table was later refined by Henry Moseley, who used X-ray spectroscopy to show that the elements should be ordered by atomic number rather than atomic mass. This discovery established the Modern  Periodic Law, which states that the physical and chemical properties of the elements are periodic  functions of their atomic numbers.5 This adjustment solved Mendeleev’s anomalies, such as the  placement of Potassium and Argon. 3.2 Modern Periodic Table  The modern periodic table is organized into horizontal rows called periods and vertical columns called  groups. There are seven periods, corresponding to the filling of the seven principal electron shells. Elements in the same group possess the same number of valence electrons and therefore exhibit similar  chemical characteristics.5 For example, Group 1 elements (Alkali Metals) are highly reactive and form   ions, while Group 17 (Halogens) are reactive non-metals that form   ions.35  The table is further divided into blocks based on orbital filling:  ● s-block: Groups 1 and 2 (and Helium). ● p-block: Groups 13 to 18. ● d-block: Transition metals (Groups 3 to 12). ● f-block: Inner transition metals (Lanthanides and Actinides), placed below the main table to  maintain a compact shape.6  3.3 Periodic Trends  Periodic trends are predictable patterns in the properties of elements as one moves across a period or  down a group. ● Atomic Radius: Decreases across a period (left to right) because the increasing nuclear charge pulls. electrons closer. It increases down a group because new shells are added.6  ● Ionization Energy: The energy required to remove an electron. It increases across a period as  electrons are more tightly held and decreases down a group due to increased shielding.5  ● Electronegativity: The ability of an atom to attract shared electrons. It increases across a period  and decreases down a group. Fluorine is the most electronegative element.5  ● Metallic Character: Decreases across a period and increases down a group.

tightly held and decreases down a group due to increased shielding.5 ● Electronegativity: The ability of an atom to attract shared electrons. It increases across a period and decreases down a group. Fluorine is the most electronegative element.5 ● Metallic Character: Decreases across a period and increases down a group. Metals are located on  the left and center, while non-metals are on the right.5  Property  Trend Across Period  Trend Down Group  Atomic Radius  Decreases  Increases  Ionization Energy  Increases  Decreases  Electronegativity  Increases  Decreases  Metallic Character  Decreases  Increases  Summary  The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, reflecting their electronic structure. Vertical  groups share chemical properties, while horizontal periods reflect shell filling. Predictable trends in size  and energy dictate the reactivity of elements. Review Questions  1. State the modern periodic law and its importance. 2. How does the electronic configuration of an element determine its position in the table?  3. Explain why the noble gases (Group 18) are chemically inert. 4. Contrast the trends of atomic radius and ionization energy across Period 3. 5. Identify the block and group for an element with electronic configuration. CHAPTER 4: Chemical Bonding  4.1 Ionic Bonding  Ionic bonding is the electrostatic force of attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by the  complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal atom to a non-metal atom.32 For example, in. the formation of Sodium Chloride  , Sodium loses its one valence electron to become a cation  , while Chlorine gains that electron to become an anion. This process allows both  atoms to achieve a stable, noble-gas electronic configuration.39  Ionic compounds do not exist as discrete molecules but as giant three-dimensional crystal lattices. These  lattices are characterized by high melting and boiling points due to the immense energy required to  overcome the strong electrostatic forces. In the solid state, ionic compounds are insulators, but they  conduct electricity when molten or dissolved in water because the ions become free to move.21 The  strength of the bond is often quantified by the lattice energy.40  4.2 Covalent Bonding  Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms.

same number of protons but differ in the number of neutrons.4 2.4 Atomic Number and Mass Number The Atomic Number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. It is the fundamental property that defines an element; every atom of Carbon, for instance, has exactly 6 protons. In a neutral  atom,   also equals the number of electrons. The Mass Number   is the total number of protons  and neutrons in the nucleus.21 The relationship is mathematically expressed as:    where   is the number of neutrons. Isotopes are atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. For example, Hydrogen  has three isotopes: Protium ( ), Deuterium ( ), and  Tritium ( ).21 The relative atomic mass   seen on the periodic table is a  weighted average of the masses of all naturally occurring isotopes of that element. Summary  Atomic theory moved from Dalton’s indestructible spheres to the quantum model. The atom consists of  a central nucleus (protons/neutrons) and outer electron orbitals. The atomic number   identifies the  element, while the mass number   and isotopes reflect nuclear variation. Review Questions  1. Contrast the atomic models proposed by Thomson and Rutherford.. 2. Define "quantization of energy" in the context of the Bohr model. 3. State the location, charge, and mass of the three subatomic particles. 4. An atom has a mass number of 35 and an atomic number of 17. Identify the element and the  number of neutrons. 5. Explain why isotopes of the same element have identical chemical properties. CHAPTER 3: Periodic Table and Periodicity  3.1 Development of the Periodic Table  The periodic table is a masterful organization of the elements that highlights the repeating nature of  their properties. Early classification attempts included Döbereiner’s Triads and Newlands’ Law of  Octaves. However, Dmitri Mendeleev is credited with the first widely accepted periodic table in 1869. Mendeleev arranged elements by increasing atomic mass and, crucially, left gaps for undiscovered  elements, predicting their properties with remarkable accuracy.33  The table was later refined by Henry Moseley, who used X-ray spectroscopy to show that the elements  should be ordered by atomic number rather than atomic mass.

and that energy is only emitted or absorbed when an electron jumps between these shells.30 2.2 Structure of the Atom The modern atom is understood to consist of a central nucleus containing protons and neutrons, which account for nearly all the atom's mass but only a fraction of its volume. Electrons occupy diffuse regions  of space around the nucleus known as orbitals.30 The arrangement of these particles is held together by  fundamental forces: the strong nuclear force binds protons and neutrons within the nucleus, while  electromagnetic attraction keeps the negative electrons bound to the positive nucleus. Quantum mechanics has replaced the idea of fixed orbits with probability-based orbitals. These orbitals describe where an electron is most likely to be found. The distribution of electrons across  these levels and sublevels is called the electronic configuration. This arrangement follows specific rules:  the Aufbau Principle (filling lowest energy levels first), Hund’s Rule (maximizing unpaired electrons in  degenerate orbitals), and the Pauli Exclusion Principle (no more than two electrons per orbital with  opposite spins).5  2.3 Subatomic Particles  The identity and behavior of an atom are determined by its three primary subatomic particles: the  proton, the neutron, and the electron. Protons and neutrons are located in the nucleus and have a  relative mass of approximately. The proton carries a positive charge of  , while the neutron  is electrically neutral. Electrons, located in the outer shells, have a negligible mass (about    of a proton) and carry a negative charge of  .21. It is the fundamental  property that defines an element; every atom of Carbon, for instance, has exactly 6 protons.

the solid state, ionic compounds are insulators, but they conduct electricity when molten or dissolved in water because the ions become free to move.21 The strength of the bond is often quantified by the lattice energy.40 4.2 Covalent Bonding Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. If the sharing is equal  (between identical atoms), the bond is non-polar. If one atom is more electronegative, it pulls the  electrons closer, creating a polar covalent bond with partial charges  .34 A special case is the  dative (coordinate) covalent bond, where one atom provides both electrons for the shared pair, as seen  in the formation of the ammonium ion  .34  Covalent substances can form simple molecular structures (like  ) with low melting points,  or giant covalent networks (like Diamond, Graphite) with extremely high melting points.38 To predict the  three-dimensional shapes of molecules, chemists use the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR)  theory. VSEPR states that electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves as far apart as  possible to minimize repulsion, resulting in geometries such as linear, trigonal planar, or tetrahedral.2  4.3 Metallic Bonding  Metallic bonding is described by the "electron sea model," where a lattice of positive metal ions  (cations) is immersed in a cloud of delocalized valence electrons. These electrons are not bound to any  single atom but are free to move throughout the entire metallic structure.34 This delocalization is the  source of the unique physical properties of metals. The mobility of the delocalized electrons explains the high thermal and electrical conductivity of metals. Furthermore, the metallic bond is non-directional, allowing the layers of cations to slide over one  another without breaking the bond when a force is applied. This accounts for the malleability and  ductility of metals.32 The strength of the metallic bond increases with the number of delocalized  electrons and the charge of the metal ions. 4.4 Intermolecular Forces  Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are the attractive forces that exist between molecules.

Consequently, a chemical equation must be balanced, with the same number of atoms of each  element on both sides.9 Balancing is achieved by adjusting coefficients—the numbers in front of the  chemical formulas. Subscripts within a formula must never be changed, as this would change the  identity of the substance.8  A systematic approach to balancing involves identifying the most complex molecule first and balancing  its atoms, then proceeding to simpler molecules, and finally checking that the total charge and atom  counts are equal on both sides.8 For instance, to balance the combustion of propane:    The coefficients   ensure 3 carbons, 8 hydrogens, and 10 oxygens on each side. 5.3 Mole Concept  The mole is the standard unit for the amount of substance, defined as the amount containing   (Avogadro’s constant) elementary entities.21 The Molar Mass   is the mass of one  mole of a substance (in  ), numerically equal to its relative atomic or molecular mass.30 The  relationship between mass   and moles   is:    For gases at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP), one mole occupies a Molar Volume of  .10 In solutions, the amount of solute is expressed in terms of Molarity  , which is  moles of solute per liter of solution.32  5.4 Stoichiometric Calculations  Stoichiometry uses the coefficients of a balanced equation as mole ratios to calculate relative quantities  of reactants and products.9 A typical problem involves three steps: (1) Convert the given mass or volume  to moles; (2) Use the mole ratio from the equation to find moles of the unknown; (3) Convert those  moles to the desired unit.8  Worked Example:  Calculate the mass of   produced when   of Aluminum reacts with excess. 1. the bond when a force is applied. This accounts for the malleability and ductility of metals.32 The strength of the metallic bond increases with the number of delocalized electrons and the charge of the metal ions. 4.4 Intermolecular Forces Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are the attractive forces that exist between molecules. While much weaker  than intramolecular bonds (ionic/covalent), IMFs determine whether a substance is a solid, liquid, or gas  at room temperature.22 The primary types include:. ● London Dispersion Forces: Temporary dipoles present in all molecules; they increase with  molecular size. ● Dipole-Dipole Interactions: Permanent attractions between the positive end of one polar molecule  and the negative end of another. ● Hydrogen Bonding: A very strong type of dipole-dipole force occurring when Hydrogen is bonded  to  , or. This is responsible for the high boiling point of water and the double-helix  structure of DNA.30  Summary  Bonding occurs through electron transfer (ionic), sharing (covalent), or delocalization (metallic). Molecular shapes are dictated by VSEPR theory. Physical states of molecular substances are governed by  the strength of intermolecular forces like hydrogen bonding. Review Questions  1. Describe the formation of an ionic bond between Magnesium   and Oxygen. 2. Use VSEPR theory to explain why   is linear while   is bent. 3. Compare the electrical conductivity of Magnesium and Magnesium Chloride. 4. Define Hydrogen bonding and discuss its effect on the physical properties of water. 5. Draw the Lewis structure for Ammonia   and identify the number of lone pairs. CHAPTER 5: Chemical Reactions and Stoichiometry  5.1 Chemical Equations  A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction, showing the reactants and the  products formed. It provides a qualitative description of the reaction components and a quantitative  measure of the amounts involved.9 To be useful, equations must include state symbols:   for solid,   for liquid,   for gas, and   for aqueous solution.9  Equations can represent various reaction types, such as combination, decomposition, single  displacement, and double displacement.3 For example, the neutralization of hydrochloric acid with  sodium hydroxide is represented as:    This net ionic equation represents the actual chemical change:  .3.

collected from these experiments, which may be qualitative or quantitative, is analyzed to draw logical conclusions. This process ensures that scientific knowledge is evidence-based and subject to rigorous verification, a mindset that chemistry teachers must instill in their students.18 Summary Chemistry is the central science studying matter and its transformations. It is divided into branches like  organic, inorganic, and physical chemistry, each playing a vital role in health, industry, and agriculture. Professionalism in chemistry requires mastery of the scientific method and a deep commitment to  laboratory safety. Review Questions  1. Define chemistry and explain its status as the "central science."  2. List and briefly describe the five main branches of chemistry. 3. Discuss three ways in which chemistry has contributed to the Nigerian economy. 4. Outline the steps of the scientific method using a hypothetical experiment. 5. Why is it important to never add water to a concentrated acid?. CHAPTER 2: Atomic Structure  2.1 Historical Development of Atomic Theory  The concept of the atom has progressed through several major milestones. John Dalton, in the early  19th century, proposed that matter consists of tiny, indestructible spheres called atoms, and that all  atoms of a given element are identical in mass and properties.21 While groundbreaking, Dalton's theory  was later modified following the discovery that atoms are divisible and that isotopes exist.21 J.J. Thomson’s experiments with cathode rays in the late 1890s revealed the existence of the electron,  leading to the "plum pudding" model, which suggested that negative electrons were embedded in a  sphere of positive charge.4  Ernest Rutherford’s gold foil experiment in 1911 provided a radical shift in perspective. He observed that  while most alpha particles passed through a thin gold sheet, some were deflected at large angles. This  led to the conclusion that the atom has a tiny, dense, positively charged center called the nucleus,  surrounded by a large volume of empty space where electrons are located.4 Niels Bohr further refined  this in 1913 by proposing that electrons move in specific, quantized circular orbits or "shells" around the  nucleus, and that energy is only emitted or absorbed when an electron jumps between these shells.30  2.2 Structure of the Atom  The modern atom is understood to consist of a central nucleus containing protons and neutrons, which  account for nearly all the atom's mass but only a fraction of its volume.
